14: The first Babylonian deportation under Nebuchadnezzar — wayyigel ʾet kol yᵉrûšālaim wᵉʾet kol haśśārîm wᵉʾet kol gibborê hāḥayil ʿăśārāh ʾălāpîm gōleh ûkol heḥārāš wᵉhammassēr lōʾ nišʾar zûlatî dallat ʿam hāʾāreṣ — removes the élite and skilled workers first: princes, warriors, craftsmen, smiths. The Hebrew gōlāh (exile, the exiled community) and its verbal root gālāh (to be uncovered, to go into exile) carry the imagery of the captive community exposed and displaced. The craftsmen (ḥārāš) are particularly significant: their removal was strategic military policy (to prevent weapon-making) but has the unintended effect of preparing a skilled exilic community who will contribute to Babylon’s cultural life (Dan 1:4-5). Isa 40:1-2 addresses the gōlāh-community: naḥămû naḥămû ʿammî, ‘Comfort, comfort my people’ — the opening address of the second Isaiah is directed precisely at those whom 2 Kgs 24:14 counted among the deported. The apostolic use of paroikia (sojourning/exile) and diaspora (dispersion) in 1 Pet 1:1, 17 and Jas 1:1 applies the exile vocabulary theologically to the church: ‘to those who are elect exiles of the Dispersion’ — the new covenant community shares the structural condition of the gōlāh, living in the world as those whose true homeland is elsewhere.
1: Bāyāmîm hāhēm (in those days) — the temporal marker tying Jehoiakim’s reign to the broader Assyrian-to-Babylonian power shift. ʿālāh nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar — Nebuchadnezzar came up — the same verb (ʿālāh) used for military campaigns throughout Kings; the king of Babylon performs the same ascending action that YHWH’s judgment-agents always perform. Jehoiakim ‘became his servant three years’ — wayyᵉhî lô ʿeḇeḏ šālōš šānîm — the vassal relation encoded in ʿeḇeḏ (servant/slave), the same word used for Israel’s slavery in Egypt. Then wayyāšob wayyimrōḏ bô — he turned and rebelled — māraḏ is the covenant-rebellion verb, used for Moab’s rebellion (3:5) and the suzerain-vassal defection formula throughout the Ancient Near East.
2: wayyšallah YHWH bô — YHWH sent against him — the šillah verb marks direct divine agency: YHWH dispatches the raiding bands as instruments of covenant judgment. The coalition of Chaldeans, Syrians, Moabites, and Ammonites are all former enemies of Israel now conscripted into service as YHWH’s punitive agents. The pattern is Deuteronomic: when Israel sins, YHWH uses surrounding nations as the covenant sword (Deut 28:25, 49-52). gĕdûdê (bands/raiding parties) — the same word used for the Syrian raiding bands of 5:2; the military form of the judgment is calibrated — irregular bands before the full siege army of ch25.
3: ʿal pî YHWH — at the mouth/command of YHWH — the fulfillment-formula the narrator uses to interpret historical events as YHWH’s direct causation. This phrase appears at key theological turning points (2 Kings 1:17; 9:26; 23:16; 24:2) where the narrator insists that political history is the execution of YHWH’s word. lᵉhasîr meʿal pānāyw — to remove from before his face — the preposition meʿal pānāyw (from before his face) is precisely the language of divine presence and its withdrawal; to be removed from before YHWH’s face is to lose covenant access. The specific grounds: bᵉḵol ḥaṭṭōʾt mᵉnaššeh — the sins of Manasseh — the narrator locates the final cause in ch21’s theological nadir.
4: wᵉgam dām hannāqî ʾăšer šāpaḵ — and also the innocent blood that he shed — dām nāqî (innocent blood) is a legal term with specific covenant weight: the shedding of innocent blood defiles the land itself and cannot be expiated except by the blood of the one who shed it (Num 35:33; Deut 19:10). The phrase waymallēʾ ʾet yᵉrûšālaim dām nāqî (he filled Jerusalem with innocent blood) — mālaʾ (filled) is the same root as YHWH filling the temple with glory; here the city is filled with judicial defilement rather than divine presence. The irreversibility — wᵉlōʾ ʾābāh YHWH lisloaḥ, YHWH was not willing to forgive — uses the verb ʾābāh (to be willing/consent), marking a divine refusal rather than an inability to forgive.
5: wᵉyeter diḇrê yᵉhôyāqîm wᵉkol ʾăšer ʿāśāh — the rest of the acts of Jehoiakim and all that he did — the standard regnal summary formula functioning here as an abrupt closure. The formula typically refers the reader to the annals of the kings of Judah; here the narrator declines to elaborate, treating Jehoiakim’s reign as having been adequately characterized by the Babylonian capitulation and the note about innocent blood. The terseness is itself evaluative: this is a king not worth extending commentary on.
6: wayyiškab yᵉhôyāqîm ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw — Jehoiakim slept with his fathers — the death formula for Judean kings, here applied without the usual notation of burial in the city of David, suggesting an undignified end (cf. Jer 22:18-19 which prophesied he would be ‘buried with the burial of a donkey’). wayyimloḵ yᵉhôyāḵîn bᵉnô taḥtāyw — Jehoiachin his son reigned in his place — the succession formula; the name Yᵉhôyāḵîn (YHWH establishes/sustains) will be crucial to the book’s closing note (25:27-30), where the Davidic seed is paradoxically ‘established’ in Babylonian exile.
7: wᵉlōʾ yāsap ʿôḏ melek miṣrayim lāṣēʾt mēʾarṣô — the king of Egypt no longer came out from his land — the imperfect with lōʾ yāsap (did not again/no more) marks a permanent geopolitical shift. The great imperial rival of Assyrian-era Judah has been neutralized: kî lāqaḥ melek bāḇel mikkaḥ naḥal miṣrayim ʿaḏ nᵉhar pĕrāt, the king of Babylon took everything from the Wadi of Egypt to the Euphrates River. The Euphrates boundary is the Abrahamic covenant promise-extent (Gen 15:18); its seizure by Babylon marks the complete reversal of the Davidic high point: what was the covenant’s eastern reach is now Babylon’s western border.
8: ben šᵉmōneh ʿeśrēh šānāh yᵉhôyāḵîn bᵉmolḵô — Jehoiachin was eighteen years old when he became king — the regnal age formula positions him as a young king (contrast v1, Jehoiakim’s longer reign). šᵉlōšāh ḥoḏāšîm mālaḵ bîrûšālāim — three months he reigned in Jerusalem — one of the shortest reigns recorded; the brevity underscores the futility of Judean kingship at this stage. The narrator uses the standard form even for a reign so short that it barely registers, maintaining the covenantal accounting structure that records every Davidic king regardless of duration.
9: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH — he did evil in the eyes of YHWH — the covenant-evaluation formula applied even to a king who reigned only three months. The formula’s consistent application regardless of reign length reflects the narrator’s insistence that every Davidic king is evaluated against the covenant standard, not merely against political outcomes. kᵉkōl ʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾāḇîw — according to all that his father had done — the continuity of apostasy through the family line; the verb ʿāśāh (did/made) links father and son in a shared moral pattern.
10: bāʿēt hahîʾ ʿālû ʿaḇdê nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar — at that time the servants of Nebuchadnezzar came up — bāʿēt hahîʾ (at that time) ties the Babylonian siege to the reign of Jehoiachin, the narrator holding timeline and theological judgment together. The plural ʿāḇdê (servants) — Nebuchadnezzar’s subordinates arrive first, before the king himself (v11). The city is put under siege — wayyābōʾ hāʿîr bammāṣôr — the word māṣôr (siege/siege-work) carries military and legal weight: the siege is the covenant curse of Deut 28:52-57 enacted in history.
11: ûnᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar melek bāḇel bāʾ ʿal hāʿîr — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came to the city — the king himself arrives, elevating the personal weight of the confrontation. The grammar puts Nebuchadnezzar in parallel with David’s capture of Jerusalem from the Jebusites (2 Sam 5:6-9): as David came against the city and took it, Nebuchadnezzar comes against David’s city. The reversal is total: the city won by the first David is being taken by the king who will end the Davidic political order. wayyiṣbᵉʾû ʿālayw ʿăḇāḏāyw — his servants were besieging it — the pluperfect-sequential indicating that the servants’ siege was already underway when the king arrived.
12: wayyēṣēʾ yᵉhôyāḵîn melek yᵉhûḏāh — Jehoiachin king of Judah went out — the verb yāṣāʾ (went out) for voluntary surrender is the exact opposite of the king’s expected role of defending or fleeing. ʿal melek bāḇel hûʾ wᵉʾimmô wᵉʿaḇāḏāyw wᵉśārāyw wᵉsārîsāyw — to the king of Babylon, he and his mother and his servants and his princes and his officials — the entire court surrenders en masse; the vocabulary itemizes the covenant-community of the king (mother, servants, princes, officials). The specific note of the king’s mother (ʾimmô) is significant: the queen-mother in Judah holds a formal institutional role, and her surrender marks the end of that office’s Davidic iteration.
13: wayyōṣēʾ miššām ʾet kol ʾôṣrôt bêt YHWH wᵉʾôṣrôt bêt hammelek — he took from there all the treasuries of the house of YHWH and the treasuries of the king’s house — the stripping of the temple treasuries is narrated with the same verb yāṣāʾ (went out, brought out) used for the king’s surrender in v12; both the king and the treasure are ‘brought out’ to Babylon. wayyᵉqaṣṣēṣ ʾet kol kᵉlê hazzāhāḇ — he cut in pieces all the vessels of gold — qāṣaṣ (cut to pieces) is a term of violent reduction; the gold vessels made by Solomon for worship are dismembered. The narrator cites Solomon’s specific decree (ʾăšer ʿāśāh šᵉlōmōh melek yiśrāʾēl bᵉhêḵal YHWH kaʾăšer dibbēr YHWH) — as YHWH had spoken — invoking the fulfillment of the prophetic word about the temple’s despoliation (1 Kings 9:7-9).
15: wayyegeleh ʾet yᵉhôyāḵîn bāḇelāh — he exiled Jehoiachin to Babylon — the gālāh (exile/uncover) verb is the central lexeme of the exile narrative: deportation as uncovering/stripping, the opposite of YHWH’s covering of his people. The inventory of the deportees is precise: the king’s mother, wives, officials, and the leaders of the land (ʾêlê hāʾāreṣ — the powerful/mighty ones of the land). The word ʾêlê (terebinths/mighty ones) is a poetic term for the élite; the exile strips the land of its most powerful. Jehoiachin will be in Babylon for 37 years before the book’s closing scene of release (25:27).
16: wᵉkol ʾanšê hehayil šiḇʿat ʾălāpîm — all the men of valor, seven thousand — the military élite deported as a security measure, consistent with Babylonian imperial policy of decapitating captured nations by removing their fighting class. wehheḥārāš wᵉhammassēr ʾelep — the craftsmen and smiths, one thousand — the artisan class deported to serve Babylonian industry and to prevent the production of weapons in Judah. The deportation of skilled workers is documented in Assyrian and Babylonian administrative texts; the narrator uses the same categories. The two classes — warriors and craftsmen — are the embodiment of the covenant people’s civilizational capacity; their exile signals the end of Judah as a functioning state.
17: wayyamliḵ melek bāḇel ʾet mattanyāh dōḏô taḥtāyw — the king of Babylon made Mattaniah his uncle king in his place — the wayyamliḵ (causative: caused to reign) marks Babylonian installation of a client king; the sovereignty now rests with Nebuchadnezzar, not with the Davidic line itself. The name-change from Mattaniah to Zedekiah — wayyāsēb ʾet šᵉmô ṣiḏqiyyāhû — is a political act: renaming a vassal was a Near Eastern assertion of suzerainty (cf. the renaming of Daniel and his companions in Dan 1:7). The new name Ṣiḏqiyyāhû (YHWH is my righteousness) is theologically ironic: the king whose reign will end in covenant catastrophe bears a name that invokes covenantal fidelity.
18: ben ʿeśrîm wᵉʾaḥat šānāh ṣiḏqiyyāhû bᵉmolḵô ûšᵉtêʿeśrēh šānāh mālaḵ bîrûšālāim — standard regnal formula for Zedekiah: 21 years old, eleven-year reign. The eleven-year detail is significant: long enough to have fostered hope but ending in the complete destruction of ch25. The mother-name (wᵉšēm ʾimmô ḥamûṭal bat yirmᵉyāhû millibᵉnāh) connects Zedekiah to the same maternal line as Jehoahaz (23:31); both share the same mother from Libnah. The family genealogy is maintained in the formulaic record even as the kingdom collapses — the covenant accounting continues.
19: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉkōl ʾăšer ʿāśāh yᵉhôyāqîm — he did evil in the eyes of YHWH according to all that Jehoiakim had done — the covenant-evaluation formula aligning Zedekiah with his predecessor. The phrase kᵉkōl ʾăšer ʿāśāh (according to all that ... had done) creates a genealogy of apostasy: Manasseh’s wickedness becomes the benchmark (23:26), and subsequent kings are measured against it and against each other in a descending spiral. The imperfect verb ʿāśāh in the formula always describes a completed, evaluated pattern of behavior, not a single act.
20: kî ʿal ʾap YHWH hāyᵉtāh bîrûšālaim wîhûḏāh ʿaḏ hiššᵉlîḵô ʾōtām mēʿal pānāyw — for through the anger of YHWH it happened to Jerusalem and Judah until he had cast them out from his presence — the verse is the theological conclusion to ch24, interpreting the entire sequence of deportations and political disasters as the outworking of divine anger (ʾap, nose/anger — the somatic Hebrew idiom for intense emotion). The verb hiššᵉlîḵô (cast out) is the expulsion-from-presence verb used for Adam and Eve in Gen 3:23-24 (gāraš), establishing exile as the eschatological form of the garden expulsion. ûṣiḏqiyyāhû māraḏ bᵉmelek bāḇel — Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon — māraḏ (rebel) setting up ch25’s catastrophe.
